content:
3 min read
Just now

--

My old commute was 52 minutes each way. Multiply that by two trips, 220 working days, and you get almost 400 hours a year spent watching brake lights. I did that math on a random Tuesday, in traffic, and something in me quietly resigned before I did.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

So I’ve been a remote developer for years now. And I have opinions.
The big promise is real. Deep work at home actually exists. Three uninterrupted hours on a hard problem is a normal remote Tuesday. In the office, it was a myth. Someone’s birthday. A standup that could’ve been a message. A tap on the shoulder for something that could’ve waited until never. In all my office years I can remember maybe four afternoons where nobody interrupted me, and two of them were holidays.
My setup escalated the way these things do. First a laptop on the kitchen table. Then a proper desk. Then a second monitor, a decent chair, noise-canceling headphones. For an apartment where the loudest thing is the fridge. I regret nothing.
Now the part remote work evangelists skip.
The open floor plan followed me home. It just changed its name to Slack. The interruptions didn’t disappear, they became asynchronous, which means they now arrive at any hour and sit there, glowing, judging me. And the green dot became presence theater. People don’t trust that you’re working, so you perform availability instead of doing work, which is the exact disease we were trying to cure.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Isolation is the cost nobody prices in. Not for me (I’m fine, the fridge and I are close), but for junior developers it’s brutal. I learned half of what I know by eavesdropping on two senior devs arguing about database indexes three desks away. You can’t eavesdrop on a thread you weren’t tagged in. Juniors in remote teams learn slower, ask less, and marinate in impostor syndrome alone. If your company hires juniors and has no real mentoring plan beyond “ping me anytime”, that’s not a plan, it’s a shrug.
Timezones deserve their own paragraph of complaining. Async collaboration works beautifully until you need a yes or no. Then a question asked at 9am gets answered at 6pm, and a decision that needed thirty seconds takes a full day, round trip. I’ve watched a two-line code review take longer than the feature itself.
And my personal lowlight, in the spirit of honesty: I once spent two hours convinced our staging environment was down. Wrote it up. Pinged people. Started a thread. It wasn’t down. My VPN was on the wrong profile and I was, technically speaking, the outage. Nobody lets me forget it, and they shouldn’t.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

So where does that leave us. Remote work made me better at programming: more focus, more energy, code written by a person instead of a zombie who just survived traffic. It made me worse at the human side, and I have to compensate on purpose: scheduling calls that have no agenda, overcommunicating in writing, actually turning the camera on sometimes. None of that happens by accident anymore. In an office, the human side was free. Remote, you pay for it with intention, and plenty of people quietly don’t.
But here’s what I’ve landed on. The remote versus office debate is mostly a decoy. The real variable is whether your company measures output or presence. Remote teams that judge you by what you ship are the best environment I’ve ever worked in. Remote teams that judge you by response speed on Slack are an open office with worse coffee and infinite surveillance.
And the return-to-office wave? If a manager needs to watch you type to believe you’re working, dragging you back won’t fix their trust problem. It’ll just give them a better view.
